Knobs and screens are also quite common now, but will we see some new physical adaptations on future keyboards? Only time will tell.

It might seem that everything that could be done with mechanical keyboards has already been done, but the market has a way of surprising us. I hope someone out there is working on the next big thing that will take the keyboard community by storm on Instagram, TikTok, and YouTube. I'm looking forward to trying these future keyboards and covering them comprehensively on XDA.

item.title: August 2024's Patch Tuesday brings in some security fixes for Windows 10 and 11
item.itunes:title: August 2024's Patch Tuesday brings in some security fixes for Windows 10 and 11
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Key Takeaways

  • Patch Tuesday brings security fixes to Windows 11, addressing vulnerabilities and bug fixes for BitLocker and lock screen issues.
  • Windows 10 also receives security updates, including fixes for BitLocker and lock screen issues seen in Windows 11.
  • Microsoft acknowledges a profile picture error in both Windows 10 and 11, working on a fix for Windows 10 users.

Well, it's that time of the month again when Microsoft releases a patch for its Windows 10 and 11 systems. Known as "Patch Tuesday," it's usually held on the second Tuesday of every month and features a big update for Microsoft's supported systems. August 2024's Patch Tuesday notes have just dropped, and by the looks of things, this round of updates is a pretty calm one.

Related
Files review: A more modern alternative to the Windows 11 File Explorer

The Files app is what I want the Windows 11 File Explorer to look like, but it's not quite perfect. It's also limited by Windows in some ways.

Patch Tuesday brings some security fixes for Windows 11

First up, let's take a look at Windows 11's Patch Tuesday. Windows 11 23H2 and 22H2 are getting patch KB5041585, while 21H2 is getting KB5041592. So, what does the patch contain? As it turns out, Microsoft keeps things succinct, with just one highlight:

  • This update addresses security issues for your Windows operating system.

As for bug fixes, this update comes with a few remedies for some annoying issues:

[Protected Process Light (PPL) protections] You can bypass them.

[Windows Kernel Vulnerable Driver Blocklist file (DriverSiPolicy.p7b)] This update adds to the list of drivers that are at risk for Bring Your Own Vulnerable Driver (BYOVD) attacks.

[BitLocker (known issue)] A BitLocker recovery screen shows when you start up your device. This occurs after you install the July 9, 2024, update. This issue is more likely to occur if device encryption is on. Go to Settings > Privacy & Security > Device encryption. To unlock your drive, Windows might ask you to enter the recovery key from your Microsoft account.

[Lock screen] This update addresses CVE-2024-38143. Because of this, the “Use my windows user account” check box is not available on the lock screen to connect to Wi-Fi.

[NetJoinLegacyAccountReuse] This update removes this registry key. For more information refer to KB5020276—Netjoin: Domain join hardening changes.

[Secure Boot Advanced Targeting (SBAT) and Linux Extensible Firmware Interface (EFI)] This update applies SBAT to systems that run Windows. This stops vulnerable Linux EFI (Shim bootloaders) from running. This SBAT update will not apply to systems that dual-boot Windows and Linux. After the SBAT update is applied, older Linux ISO images might not boot. If this occurs, work with your Linux vendor to get an updated ISO image.

read more

Unfortunately, if you're running Windows 11 21H2, this update may make it so you can't change your profile picture, choosing instead to throw a 0x80070520 error. Microsoft states that if you encounter this, you should contact support.

Patch Tuesday also introduces security fixes to Windows 10

If you're using Windows 10, Patch Tuesday will bring KB5041580 to versions 22H2 and 21H2. Sure enough, August's patch was pretty quiet for Windows 10 too, as there's just one highlight:

  • This update addresses security issues for your Windows operating system.

The update also addresses some issues that also saw fixes in Windows 11:

[BitLocker (known issue)] A BitLocker recovery screen shows when you start up your device. This occurs after you install the July 9, 2024, update. This issue is more likely to occur if device encryption is on. Go to Settings > Privacy & Security > Device encryption. To unlock your drive, Windows might ask you to enter the recovery key from your Microsoft account.

[Lock screen] This update addresses CVE-2024-38143. Because of this, the “Use my windows user account” check box is not available on the lock screen to connect to Wi-Fi.

[NetJoinLegacyAccountReuse] This update removes this registry key. For more information refer to KB5020276—Netjoin: Domain join hardening changes.

[Secure Boot Advanced Targeting (SBAT) and Linux Extensible Firmware Interface (EFI)] This update applies SBAT to systems that run Windows. This stops vulnerable Linux EFI (Shim bootloaders) from running. This SBAT update will not apply to systems that dual-boot Windows and Linux. After the SBAT update is applied, older Linux ISO images might not boot. If this occurs, work with your Linux vendor to get an updated ISO image.

read more

Microsoft states that the 0x80070520 bug involving changing your profile picture also appears in Windows 10. However, unlike the advice Microsoft gave for Windows 11, the company states that it's working on a fix.

If you want to download the update or check out the notes, here are all the details:

Windows version

Build number

Changelog

Supported editions

Manual Download

Windows 11 23H2

22631.4037

KB5041585

All editions

Microsoft Update Catalog

Windows 11 22H2

22621.4037

KB5041585

All editions

Microsoft Update Catalog

Windows 11 21H2

22000.3147

KB5041592

All editions

Microsoft Update Catalog

Windows 10 22H2

19045.4780

KB5040427

All editions

Microsoft Update Catalog

Windows 10 21H2

19044.4780

KB5040427

Windows 10 Enterprise LTSC 2021, Windows 10 IoT Enterprise LTSC 2021

Microsoft Update Catalog

Related
Windows 11 update tracker: Download and install the latest update

We're tracking all the Windows 11 updates for you.

item.title: Google Drive vs. Dropbox: Which cloud storage is right for you?
item.itunes:title: Google Drive vs. Dropbox: Which cloud storage is right for you?
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Key Takeaways

  • Both Google Drive and Dropbox offer cross-platform availability and customizable user interfaces with dark mode options.
  • Google Drive integrates seamlessly with Google apps & offers AI features, while Dropbox excels in PDF editing, screen recording, and productivity add-ons.
  • Dropbox surpasses Google Drive in privacy features for sharing & collaboration, but Google Drive is more cost-effective for larger storage plans.

The cloud storage market is quite crowded, and picking the right service can be a daunting task. Among the myriad of options available, Google Drive and Dropbox stand out as two of the most popular and reliable services. But which one truly deserves your trust and data? In this post, we will compare them based on user interface, features, price, and sharing experience to help you make an informed decision.

Related
Should you use Google Drive, OneDrive, Dropbox, or Nextcloud?

Analyzing top cloud storage solutions: A deep dive into Google Drive, OneDrive, Dropbox, and Nextcloud

Cross-platform availability

Whether you choose Dropbox or Google Drive, cross-platform availability shouldn’t be an issue. Both services are available on Windows, Mac, iPhone, Android, iPad, and, of course, the web. Unlike Google Drive, Dropbox also has a native app on Linux.

User interface

Keeping in line with other Google apps and services, Google Drive recently received a much-needed Material You makeover on the web. Google Drive also features a brand-new Home menu which shows suggested files, folders, and chips at the top to help you identify relevant ones in no time.

Dropbox also offers a neat-looking Home menu with suggestions at the top and all your files and folders at the bottom. Both services support a handy dark mode and several customization options to change the appearance of unnecessary menus on the main page.

Between the two, we will give an edge to Dropbox due to a dedicated Photos tab that lets you check all of your media uploads in a single place. With Google Drive, you must visit Google Photos separately.

Google Drive vs. Dropbox: Features

Let’s go over some of the noteworthy features of Google Drive and Dropbox. We will first start with Google Drive.

Customization

Google Drive is quite customizable. For instance, if you prefer the old behavior, you can revert to the previous layout from Settings. You can even change density and open PDFs in preview or a new tab. Another neat trick is the ability to change the folder color for better differentiation.

Integration with Google apps and services

This shouldn’t come as a surprise. Google Drive has a seamless integration with all the company’s apps and services. For instance, you can create new Docs, Sheets, Slides, and Forms right from the top menu. You can also access your Google Calendar entries, Keep notes, tasks, and contacts from the sidebar.

Android and Chromebook backup

If you are an Android or Chromebook user, you can back up your messages, app data, call history, and a lot more with your Drive account. When you switch to another Android device, you can simply restore the data from Google Drive and complete the setup in no time.

Gemini AI

If you have a Google One AI Premium plan, explore Gemini AI in Google Drive. It can give you brief information about a file, summarize a topic, answer a question, and you can enter prompts to get back what you are looking for.

For example, you can simply select a PDF or Google Docs file and click Ask Gemini to view a quick summary on the sidebar.

Now, let’s go over some of the trendy features of Dropbox.

PDF editor

Dropbox comes with a feature-rich PDF editor. You can draw over it, use the highlight tool, add text, comment, self-sign, and even send it for signature. You won’t even need a separate PDF editor. In comparison, Google Drive has a built-in bare-bones PDF viewer.

Screen recorder

Dropbox packs in a useful screen recorder tool to record, save, and collaborate on clips. Capture can come in handy when you want to record an educational course or give an overview of a new app to your employees or students.

Productivity add-ons

Dropbox has an ecosystem of services and apps to elevate your workflow in no time. For example, Dropbox Paper is a capable alternative to Google Docs to help you draft perfect documents. Passwords provides another robust option to save your login entries and account information securely across devices. DocSend lets you send documents securely and track activity in real-time. There is also a Backup tool to back up all your devices automatically.

Dropbox automation

Dropbox also supports automation, where you can set rules to rename files, convert them to PDFs, convert images to specific formats, and even auto-unzip files. It streamlines your repetitive tasks and boosts your productivity.

Related
5 reasons Microsoft OneDrive is better than Google Drive

Microsoft’s cloud storage platform is worth considering over Google Drive. Here's why it works well for me.

Sharing and collaboration

Here is where Dropbox truly marches ahead of Google Drive. With Dropbox, you can password-protect a link, set an expiration date, and even disable downloads in a shared link. These are some of the robust privacy features to keep prying eyes away from your confidential files.

With Google Drive, you can only change the participant’s role – Viewer, Commenter, or Editor. There is no built-in option to password-protect or set expiry on your generated links.

Google Drive vs. Dropbox: Price

Google offers a generous free plan to get started. You get 15GB of free space that’s divided between Drive, Photos, and Gmail. Dropbox only offers 2GB of free space. As for the pricing, both are similarly priced at $10 per month for 2TB of cloud space. Unlike Dropbox, Google Drive also offers cheaper Google One plans, like lower-tiers of 100GB space for $2 per month and 200GB space for $3 per month. Google One will also unlock premium media editing features for you in Google Photos.

Clash of the cloud Titans

Whether you are looking for seamless collaboration, robust security, or ample storage for your media files, understanding the strengths and weaknesses of these two cloud giants is crucial. Google Drive has flawless integration with company products, Gemini AI, and customization, while Dropbox hits back with useful productivity add-ons, a robust PDF editor, specific privacy settings, and automation. Pick a platform that best aligns with your requirements and lifestyle.

OneDrive, Microsoft’s cloud storage solution, is another capable option with a robust integration with Windows. Check out our comparison guide to learn the differences between OneDrive and Dropbox.

Gemini AI

If you have a Google One AI Premium plan, explore Gemini AI in Google Drive. It can give you brief information about a file, summarize a topic, answer a question, and you can enter prompts to get back what you are looking for.

For example, you can simply select a PDF or Google Docs file and click Ask Gemini to view a quick summary on the sidebar.

Now, let’s go over some of the trendy features of Dropbox.

PDF editor

Dropbox comes with a feature-rich PDF editor. You can draw over it, use the highlight tool, add text, comment, self-sign, and even send it for signature. You won’t even need a separate PDF editor. In comparison, Google Drive has a built-in bare-bones PDF viewer.

Screen recorder

Dropbox packs in a useful screen recorder tool to record, save, and collaborate on clips. Capture can come in handy when you want to record an educational course or give an overview of a new app to your employees or students.

Productivity add-ons

Dropbox has an ecosystem of services and apps to elevate your workflow in no time. For example, Dropbox Paper is a capable alternative to Google Docs to help you draft perfect documents. Passwords provides another robust option to save your login entries and account information securely across devices. DocSend lets you send documents securely and track activity in real-time. There is also a Backup tool to back up all your devices automatically.

Dropbox automation

Dropbox also supports automation, where you can set rules to rename files, convert them to PDFs, convert images to specific formats, and even auto-unzip files. It streamlines your repetitive tasks and boosts your productivity.

Sharing and collaboration

Here is where Dropbox truly marches ahead of Google Drive. With Dropbox, you can password-protect a link, set an expiration date, and even disable downloads in a shared link. These are some of the robust privacy features to keep prying eyes away from your confidential files.

With Google Drive, you can only change the participant’s role – Viewer, Commenter, or Editor. There is no built-in option to password-protect or set expiry on your generated links.

Google Drive vs. Dropbox: Price

Google offers a generous free plan to get started. You get 15GB of free space that’s divided between Drive, Photos, and Gmail. Dropbox only offers 2GB of free space. As for the pricing, both are similarly priced at $10 per month for 2TB of cloud space. Unlike Dropbox, Google Drive also offers cheaper Google One plans, like lower-tiers of 100GB space for $2 per month and 200GB space for $3 per month. Google One will also unlock premium media editing features for you in Google Photos.

Clash of the cloud Titans

Whether you are looking for seamless collaboration, robust security, or ample storage for your media files, understanding the strengths and weaknesses of these two cloud giants is crucial. Google Drive has flawless integration with company products, Gemini AI, and customization, while Dropbox hits back with useful productivity add-ons, a robust PDF editor, specific privacy settings, and automation. Pick a platform that best aligns with your requirements and lifestyle.

OneDrive, Microsoft’s cloud storage solution, is another capable option with a robust integration with Windows. Check out our comparison guide to learn the differences between OneDrive and Dropbox.
